epigraph.text:
For everything there is a season, and a time for every 
matter under heaven.

content.text:
Consider a vending machine. You press a button—say, **B3**—and out 
drops a bag of pretzels. Every time you press **B3**, you get pretzels. 
Not chips, not a candy bar, not nothing at all: pretzels, always pretzels. 
Each button corresponds to exactly one product. You would never trust a 
machine that dispensed a different snack every time you pressed the same 
button.

This simple expectation—*one input, one definite output*—is the 
heart of one of the most important ideas in all of mathematics. It is the 
idea of a **function**, and it will be the language in which we write 
every theorem, every formula, and every calculation in this course.
Functions as Mappings

Functions are everywhere in daily life, even when we do not call them by 
name. The scanner at the grocery store assigns a price to each barcode. 
Your phone's GPS assigns a location to each moment in time. The tax code 
assigns a tax owed to each level of income. In every case, a rule takes an 
*input* and produces exactly one *output*.
Let us give this idea a precise name.

content.title: Function
content.content:
A **function** $f$ from a set $A$ to a set 
$B$ is a rule that assigns to each element $x$ in $A$ exactly one element, 
called $f(x)$, in $B$. The set $A$ is called the 
**domain** of $f$. The set of all output values 
$\{f(x) : x \in A\}$ is called the **range** of $f$.

content.text: Two features of this definition deserve emphasis:

items:
**Every** element of the domain must have an output. The 
 function cannot "skip" an input.

Each input produces **exactly one** output. A single input 
 cannot yield two different outputs.

content.text:
Notice what the definition does *not* require: it does not say that 
different inputs must produce different outputs. The function 
$f(x) = x^{2}$ sends both $3$ and $-3$ to $9$, and that is perfectly 
acceptable. What matters is that each input has *one* output, not that 
each output comes from one input.

A mapping diagram like [fig:mapping-diagram] makes the definition 
visual: every element on the left has exactly one arrow leaving it.

content.title: Deciding whether a rule is a function
content.problem:
Which of the following rules define functions from the given domain to $\R$?

1. The rule that assigns to each real number $x$ the value $\sqrt{x}$. 
 Domain: $\R$.

2. The rule that assigns to each nonneg\-ative real number $x$ the 
 value $\sqrt{x}$. Domain: $[0, \infty)$.

3. The rule that assigns to each person their biological mother. 
 Domain: all living people.
content.solution:
1. **Not a function** on the stated domain. The input $x = -4$ 
 has no real-number output, so the rule fails to assign a value to every 
 element of $\R$.

2. **Yes, a function.** Every nonneg\-ative number has exactly one 
 nonneg\-ative square root. The domain $[0,\infty)$ has been chosen so 
 that each input produces exactly one output.

3. **Yes, a function.** Every person has exactly one biological 
 mother. (Two people may share the same mother—that is fine; the 
 definition only requires uniqueness of output for each input.)

content.text: Function Notation

When we write $f(x) = 3x + 5$, we are doing two things at once: naming the 
function ($f$) and giving the rule ("multiply the input by $3$ and add $5$"). 
The symbol $f(x)$ is read "$f$ of $x$" and denotes *the output of $f$ 
when the input is $x$*.

content.title: The parentheses in $f(x)$ do not mean multiplication
content.content:
The notation $f(x)$ does **not** mean "$f$ times $x$." The 
parentheses indicate *function evaluation*: we are feeding $x$ into 
the rule $f$. This distinction will matter enormously when we study the 
product of two functions later in this chapter.

content.text:
To **evaluate** a function at a particular input, we substitute that 
value for $x$ everywhere it appears and simplify.

content.title: Evaluating a function
content.problem:
Let $f(x) = x^{2} - 4x + 7$. Find each of the following.

1. $f(3)$

2. $f(-2)$

3. $f(a+1)$

content.text:
Part (c) above is especially important. In calculus we will frequently 
evaluate functions at expressions like $x + h$, $a + \Delta x$, or 
$\cos\theta$. The key skill is *mechanical substitution*: replace the 
variable with the given expression and simplify.

content.title: The difference quotient
content.problem:
Let $f(x) = x^{2}$. Compute and simplify the **difference quotient**
\[
 \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}, \qquad h \neq 0.
\]

content.text: Domain and Range

When a function is given by a formula without a stated domain, we adopt the 
convention that the domain is the **natural domain**: 
the largest set of real numbers for which the formula produces a real-number 
output.

content.title: Finding the Natural Domain
content.content:
To find the natural domain of a function given by a formula:

1. Start with all real numbers: $(-\infty, \infty)$.

2. **Exclude** values that create division by zero (set each 
 denominator $\neq 0$).

3. **Exclude** values that produce a negative number under an 
 even root (set the radicand $\geq 0$).

4. **Exclude** values that produce a negative or zero argument in 
 a logarithm (set the argument $> 0$).

content.title: Finding natural domains
content.problem:
Find the domain of each function.

1. $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x^{2} - 9}$

2. $g(x) = \sqrt{5 - x}$

3. $h(x) = \dfrac{\sqrt{x+3}}{x-1}$
content.solution:
1. We need $x^{2} - 9 \neq 0$, so $x \neq \pm 3$. The domain is 
 $(-\infty,-3) \cup (-3,3) \cup (3,\infty)$.

2. We need $5 - x \geq 0$, so $x \leq 5$. The domain is 
 $(-\infty, 5]$.

3. We need *both* $x + 3 \geq 0$ (for the square root) *and* 
 $x - 1 \neq 0$ (for the denominator). From the first condition, 
 $x \geq -3$. From the second, $x \neq 1$. The domain is 
 $[-3, 1) \cup (1, \infty)$.

content.text:
The **range** of a function is often harder to determine. For a 
function given by a graph, the range is the set of $y$-values that actually 
appear on the graph—read it off the vertical axis. For a formula, we 
sometimes must reason about what outputs are possible.

content.title: Finding the range from a formula
content.solution:
Since $x^{2} \geq 0$ for all real $x$, we have $f(x) = x^{2} + 1 \geq 1$. 
Moreover, $f(0) = 1$, so the value $1$ is achieved. As $x \to \pm\infty$, 
$f(x) \to \infty$, so every value $\geq 1$ is in the range. The range is 
$[1, \infty)$.

content.text: Four Ways to Represent a Function

A function can be described in four ways, and a good mathematician moves 
fluently among all of them.

items:
**Verbally** (a description in words): "$T$ is the temperature 
 in Phoenix at time $t$ hours after midnight."

**Numerically** (a table of values): recording temperature 
 readings every hour gives a table of $(t, T)$ pairs.

**Visually** (a graph): plotting the table produces a curve in 
 the $tT$-plane.

**Algebraically** (an explicit formula): $T(t) = 75 + 
 20\sin\!\bigl(\frac{\pi}{12}(t - 14)\bigr)$ might approximate the 
 daily temperature cycle.

content.text:
Not every function comes with a tidy formula. The temperature function above 
is perfectly well-defined even if we can only measure it from a thermometer 
and record it in a table. In calculus, we will often study functions that 
are defined by physical processes—position, velocity, population—and 
learn to extract information from whichever representation is available.
The Vertical Line Test

Not every curve in the $xy$-plane is the graph of a function. A circle, 
for instance, assigns two $y$-values to most $x$-values. The following 
test makes this visual.

content.title: The Vertical Line Test
content.content:
A curve in the $xy$-plane is the graph of a function of $x$ if and only if 
no vertical line intersects the curve more than once.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The reasoning is direct: a vertical line at $x = a$ collects all points 
$(a, y)$ on the curve. If there is more than one such point, the curve 
assigns multiple $y$-values to the input $a$, violating the definition of 
a function.
Piecewise-Defined Functions

Sometimes a single formula cannot capture the behavior of a function across 
its entire domain. In such cases, we use different formulas on different 
parts of the domain.

content.title: Piecewise-Defined Function
content.content:
A **piecewise-defined function** uses 
different formulas for different intervals of its domain:
\[
 f(x) = \begin{cases}
 \text{formula}_1, & \text{if } x \in I_1, \\
 \text{formula}_2, & \text{if } x \in I_2, \\
 \;\;\vdots & \\
 \text{formula}_n, & \text{if } x \in I_n.
 \end{cases}
\]
The intervals $I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_n$ must not overlap (to preserve the 
"exactly one output" requirement) and together they form the domain.

content.title: Evaluating a piecewise function
content.problem:
Let
\[
 f(x) = \begin{cases}
 x + 2, & \text{if } x < 0, \\
 x^{2}, & \text{if } 0 \leq x \leq 3, \\
 2x + 3, & \text{if } x > 3.
 \end{cases}
\]
Find $f(-1)$, $f(0)$, $f(2)$, and $f(5)$.
content.solution:
- $f(-1)$: Since $-1 < 0$, use the first piece: 
 $f(-1) = (-1) + 2 = 1$.

- $f(0)$: Since $0 \leq 0 \leq 3$, use the second piece: 
 $f(0) = 0^{2} = 0$.

- $f(2)$: Since $0 \leq 2 \leq 3$, use the second piece: 
 $f(2) = 2^{2} = 4$.

- $f(5)$: Since $5 > 3$, use the third piece: 
 $f(5) = 2(5) + 3 = 13$.

content.title: The absolute value function
content.problem:
The **absolute value function** is a 
piecewise function hiding in plain sight:
\[
 \abs{x} = \begin{cases}
 x, & \text{if } x \geq 0, \\
 -x, & \text{if } x < 0.
 \end{cases}
\]
Its graph forms a "V" shape, with vertex at the origin. We will encounter 
this function again when we study limits, because its sharp corner presents 
a challenge for differentiation.

content.title: Peter Gustav Lejeune Dirichlet (1805--1859)
content.content:
The modern definition of a function—as a rule assigning to each input 
exactly one output, with no requirement of a single formula—is largely 
due to Dirichlet. Before him, mathematicians often assumed that "function" 
meant "given by a formula." Dirichlet showed that the concept is far more 
general: even a rule with no closed-form expression can be a perfectly valid 
function. His insight freed mathematics from the tyranny of formulas and 
opened the door to the rigorous analysis that underlies modern calculus.

content.text: Looking Ahead

Functions are the vocabulary we will use to describe *change*. In 
[ch:limits] we will ask: what happens to $f(x)$ as $x$ approaches a 
particular value? In [ch:derivative] we will measure how fast $f(x)$ 
is changing at a single instant. In later chapters we will learn to 
accumulate those changes into areas, volumes, and totals. Every one of 
these ideas begins with a function.

The rest of this chapter surveys the functions you will need—polynomials, 
exponentials, logarithms, trigonometric functions—and reviews the algebra 
of combining, transforming, and inverting them. Think of what follows as 
assembling a toolkit: the richer your collection of functions, the more 
phenomena you can model and the more problems you can solve.

content.caption: A mapping diagram showing a function. Each element in the domain has exactly one arrow pointing to an element in the codomain.
content.alt: A mapping diagram with two ovals. The left oval labeled 'Domain' contains elements a, b, c. The right oval labeled 'Codomain' contains elements 1, 2, 3. Arrows connect each domain element to exactly one codomain element, illustrating that every input maps to exactly one output.

content.caption: The graph of the absolute value function $f(x) = |x|$.
content.alt: A V-shaped graph of the absolute value function. The graph has a vertex at the origin, with the left branch going up and to the left with slope -1, and the right branch going up and to the right with slope 1.

content.caption: The vertical line test. A curve represents a function if and only if every vertical line intersects it at most once.
content.alt: Two graphs side by side. The left graph shows a curve that passes the vertical line test - a dashed vertical line crosses it only once. The right graph shows a circle that fails the test - a vertical line crosses it twice.

marginNotes.text:
As the rain and the snow come down from 
heaven, and do not return to it without watering the earth and making it bud 
and flourish…\ so is my word that goes out from my mouth: it will not 
return to me empty.

If I rise on the wings of the dawn, if I 
settle on the far side of the sea, even there your hand will guide me, your 
right hand will hold me fast.

For everything there is a season: 
a time to weep, and a time to laugh; a time to mourn, and a time to dance.

marginNotes.author: Leonhard Euler
marginNotes.text:
For the sake of brevity, we will represent this 
function of $x$ by the symbol $f(x)$.

marginNotes.author: Peter Dirichlet
marginNotes.text:
In general, the function $y$ is to be taken 
such that for each value of $x$ there corresponds a unique value of $y$.
